message:
fix(types): PathMapping::apply emits exactly one separator at the splice

When both the mapping's `to` prefix ended with a path separator AND
the matched `rest` began with one — e.g. PathMapping { from: "/a",
to: "/b/" }.apply("/a/file") — the old branch-shape concatenated
them verbatim and produced "/b//file". The previous code only
inserted an explicit separator when `from` had ended with one, and
otherwise fell through to the plain `"{to}{rest}"` concatenation,
so this double-sep output was reachable for any combination where
only `to` carried the trailing sep.

Double separators canonicalize identically under POSIX, so the bug
never broke filesystem operations. It did break downstream
string-based path-equality consumers: workspace matching, cache
keys, and export manifests compared raw strings and saw
"/b//file" vs "/b/file" as distinct paths.

Rewrite apply() around an explicit (to_ends_with_sep,
rest_starts_with_sep) truth table:

    to_sep  rest_sep  action
    ------  --------  --------------------------------------------
    true    true      drop the leading sep from rest → to + rest[1..]
    true    false     concatenate — to already has the sep
    false   true      concatenate — rest already has the sep
    false   false     insert a sep; `from` must have carried one
                      (enforced by the boundary check), so reuse its
                      flavor ('\\' if from ends with backslash, '/'
                      otherwise) for cross-platform mappings

The leading boundary check is also simplified into its positive
form: if neither `from` ends with a separator nor `rest` starts
with one, the match was a filename-substring hit (e.g. from="/a"
vs path="/ab") and we return None early.

Conformance tests in src/connectors/conformance_tests.rs are
extended to pin the contract:

- path_mapping: adds one case per (from_sep, to_sep) combination
  plus Windows-style backslash variants; the regression case
  ("/a", "/b/", "/a/file", Some("/b/file")) is explicitly labeled.
- path_mapping_emits_single_separator_at_splice: new property test
  that sweeps every shape × realistic POSIX/Windows input and
  asserts the output never contains a doubled separator (guarding
  against future regressions without requiring exhaustive case
  enumeration).

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
